body_txt: A lot of tropes have origins way back when media was still forming; this is one of them. It might have been an early way to appeal to the masses, or just due to the way aristocrats tended to look down on the general public. But it was then and is now really popular to cast characters with Knight Fever titles as villainous, malevolent, or evil. note Ironically, the word "villain" comes from the Medieval Latin word for villager or peasant, and was used derisively by the aristocrats of feudal Europe. A variety of Meaningful Titles exist — people with feudal titles are very commonly evil. Popular titles are Count and Baron — two titles which are rarely seen on a good guy. (Oddly enough, a Countess has a better-than-even chance of being a decent woman. A Baroness , on the other hand, is so likely to be evil, she has her own trope. ) And though it doesn't show up a great deal, you should break out in a cold sweat when you meet a Viscount . Interestingly, both counts and barons are fairly common titles among Continental Europeans, but in England (and Scotland and Wales and Ireland) the title "count" doesn’t exist, instead replaced by "earl" (who is usually depicted as a stodgy but typically benevolent patriarch). Meanwhile, although "baron" is a common title in the British Peerage—the most common, in fact—the word is only ever used in formal contexts, and British barons are pretty much always just called "Lord". As for viscounts, they really are comparatively rare in Britain (as the Viscount Melbourne once explained to Queen Victoria ). This may suggest a regional bias in which aristocrats are cast as villains. For more discussion on how the degree of villainy differs depending on the kind of aristocrat a character is, see the Analysis page . While evil, aristocrats are often willing to be polite and even with people of their own rank. Moral Myopia , however, often limits it to fellow aristocrats. Commoners are just out of luck — especially servants . If they're not actively in charge, expect them to be part of the Omniscient Council of Vagueness . Very prone to Screw the Rules, I Have Money! , Screw the Rules, I Make Them! , and Screw the Rules, I Have Connections! . Sometimes, even prone to Screw the Money, I Have Rules! . In many cases, their fine dressings can also be a reflection of their rotten hearts. Related tropes include The Baroness , The Caligula , Decadent Court , Eat the Rich , Kill the Poor , Hates Rich People , Slobs Versus Snobs , and The Von Trope Family . For the modern version of this trope, see Corrupt Corporate Executive , and to a lesser extent, Nouveau Riche . No Real Life Examples, Please! Not to be confused with The Aristocrats , a "stock joke" based on this premise.
